title: Google Reverses Annoying Android UI Change in Major Update, Reviving Fan Favorite Feature
body:
In a move that is sure to bring relief to Android users, Google has partially reversed one of the most contentious changes introduced in Android 12. The company has reintroduced the option to control Wi-Fi and mobile data separately, a feature that was merged into a single "Internet" tile in the previous version of the operating system. This change, which was first spotted on the Canary channel, marks a significant shift in Google's approach to user interface design.

The unified Internet tile, introduced in Android 12, was intended to simplify the process of managing internet connectivity on Android devices. However, it quickly became clear that this change was not without its drawbacks. Many users found that the lack of separate toggles for Wi-Fi and mobile data made it difficult to quickly switch between different types of internet connections, particularly in situations where Wi-Fi was not available. Furthermore, the new design also removed the option to easily toggle data roaming, a feature that was previously easily accessible.

Google's decision to reintroduce separate toggles for Wi-Fi and mobile data is a significant acknowledgment of user feedback. It appears that the company has taken the criticisms of its previous design to heart and is working to create a more user-friendly experience for Android users. This move is also consistent with Google's stated goal of providing a more intuitive and customizable interface for its devices. By giving users the option to control Wi-Fi and mobile data separately, Google is enabling them to tailor their experience to their specific needs.

The reintroduction of separate toggles for Wi-Fi and mobile data is also significant in the context of the ongoing evolution of Android. As the operating system continues to evolve and improve, it is clear that Google is committed to listening to user feedback and making adjustments accordingly. This approach is in line with the company's stated goal of creating a more open and collaborative approach to software development, one that prioritizes user needs and preferences.

It remains to be seen whether Google will continue to roll out this change to other versions of Android, including the stable channel. However, the fact that the company is already testing this feature on the Canary channel suggests that it is serious about making this change a permanent part of the Android experience. As Android continues to evolve and improve, it will be interesting to see how Google balances the needs of different user groups and makes design decisions that benefit the broadest possible range of users.

In conclusion, Google's decision to reintroduce separate toggles for Wi-Fi and mobile data is a welcome move that reflects the company's commitment to user feedback and satisfaction. As Android continues to evolve and improve, it will be exciting to see how Google's design decisions shape the user experience and meet the needs of its growing user base.
